---
title: "02_TimeAlignment"
author: "Rhiannon Nolan"
date: "2026-04-14"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = F)
```

```{r}
#install necessary packages
if (!require(rstudioapi)){install.packages("rstudioapi")}
me <- (rstudioapi::getActiveDocumentContext()$path) #get current file name
wd <- dirname(me)

if (!require(renv)){ install.packages("renv")} #use renv to find package dependencies
if(!require(iNEXT)){remotes::install_version("iNEXT", version = "2.0.20")} #because I use an old version of iNEXT
packs <- unique(renv::dependencies(me)$Package) #list of packages needed for this document
for (i in seq_along(packs)){
  if (!require(packs[i], character.only=T)){ #install any missing packs
    install.packages(packs[i])
  }
}
lapply(packs, library, character.only=T) #load packages
rm(me, i) #clean env
```

### This markdown assumes you've run the code in 01_Data_Compilation.Rmd

```{r}
#if needed, load saved objects
load(paste0(wd, "/Data/comp_dat.RData"))
#should load the following:
#wk_data = data frame of my measured isotope data
#test_data = list of sections from wk_data
#ref_dat = data frame of other people's data with biozones
#align_data = list of sections from ref_dat
```

```{r}
#Hay, C.C., et al., 2019, A library of early Cambrian chemostratigraphic correlations from a reproducible algorithm: Geology, v. 47, p. 457–460, https://doi.org/10.1130/G46019.1
#Using algorithm to align columns 

#They use a complex version of dynamic time warping. For this project, I use a simplified dtw using the r package 'dtw'
library(dtw)
#https://dynamictimewarping.github.io/

alignment <- list() #list in which i will put dtw objects

#goal: make dtw objects of each comparison between my data and reference data sections
for( i in seq_along (test_data)){ #start out with for loop going through each of my sections
  og_name <- names(test_data)[i] #section in my data
  y <- test_data[[i]]$d13C #d13C data that i'm aligning to the dated columns
  
  for (j in seq_along(align_data)){ #for loop going through each of the reference sections
    x <- align_data[[j]]$d13C #d13C that is the reference to which my data is aligned
    name <- paste0(og_name, "_", names(align_data)[j])
    
    wiggle <- dtw(x = x, y = y, #compute alignment using dtw
                  step.pattern = asymmetric,
                  open.end = T,
                  open.begin = T)
    
    alignment[[name]] <- wiggle #save this dtw object in the list "alignment"
    
  }
  
}

rm(wiggle, og_name, i, j, x, y, name) #clean env
```

#Get alignment index from each dtw object
```{r}
library(stringr)
#integrate the different alignments to find the best one for each section
#names(test_data) #these are the sections I tested

#the dtw object creates indices where x[i] = y[j] so the two columns can be aligned
indexed_ref <- align_data #data frame for data with indices
for (i in seq_along(indexed_ref)){
  indexed_ref[[i]]$ref_index <- seq_along(indexed_ref[[i]]$meter)} #add reference index value
rm(i)
#while we are here, do the same for the test_data
indexed_test <- test_data
for (i in seq_along(indexed_test)){
  indexed_test[[i]]$index <- seq_along(indexed_test[[i]]$meter)} #add reference index value
rm(i)


#extract the index1 (reference index) and index2 (my data index) for each dtw object
for(i in seq_along(names(test_data))){
  name <- names(test_data)[i] #name of section
  subset <- alignment[grepl(paste0(name), names(alignment))] #subset to just alignments of this section
  
  for (j in seq_along(names(subset))){
    #extract other data from indexed_ref, where the order is the same
    indexed_ref[[j]]$subset_ind <- subset[[j]]$index2 #index values for test data 
    colnames(indexed_ref[[j]])[ncol(indexed_ref[[j]])] <- name #rename the last col with the section its for
  }
}

rm(i,j,name,subset)
```

## 
```{r}

```


```{r}
#align to accurate ages
#extract accurate timings from sections
#line up my sections using dtw indices
```


```{r}
save(test_data, align_data, ref_dat, wk_data, alignment, 
     file = paste0(wd, "/Data/align_dat.RData")) #save data objects we will use later
```
